meta.title: S203 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes S203 engine — OM611, OM646, M271, M112, M272, M113. Real UK costs, the 'Black Death' warning, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C‑Class S203 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S203 engine typically costs £1,200–£8,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume OM646 diesel sits at £1,800–£3,200, while the C 55 AMG M113 V8 ranges up to £8,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes S203 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112 V6 (C 240 / C 320) — robust, smooth, and free of the M271's camshaft adjuster issues and the M272's balance shaft concerns. The OM646 diesel is also a strong performer with proven 200,000+ mile potential when maintained correctly.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes C‑Class S203?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector seal failure ('Black Death') on the OM611/OM646 diesel, camshaft adjuster wear on the M271 supercharged petrol, balance shaft sprocket wear on the M272 V6, valve cover gaskets and timing chain wear on the M113 V8, and timing chain tensioner wear on the M112 V6.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C‑Class S203 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and maintenance history. The M112 V6 and OM646 diesel are the two most reliable options. The M272 V6 carries a known balance shaft risk — check history before buying. The M271 is improved over the older M111 but still has camshaft adjuster issues. The M113 V8 is reliable given its power output but demands meticulous maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes S203 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a high‑mileage M272 V6 (C 230 / C 350) without documented balance shaft history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected OM611/OM646 diesel with visible carbon around the injectors is the second.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my S203 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Tapping sound from the injector area (OM611/OM646 'Black Death'), timing chain rattle on start‑up (M271), check engine light with camshaft position codes (M272), or valve cover oil leaks and timing wear (M113) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes C‑Class S203 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £6,000 S203, a £3,200 OM646 recon is 53% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £3,500 S203, the same recon is over 90% — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes S203 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM646 diesel and M112 V6 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The M271's limiting factor is the camshaft adjuster — addressed proactively, it can reach 150,000+ miles. The M272's balance shaft is the limiting factor — repaired, it's a durable engine. The M113 V8 is capable of high mileage but costs escalate.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the S203, W203 and CL203?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the second‑generation C‑Class platform and most engines. The S203 is the 5‑door estate (this page, 2001–2007), the W203 is the 4‑door saloon (2001–2007), and the CL203 is the 3‑door SportCoupé (2001–2005). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes S203 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, on the engine block itself, and in the service book. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes C‑Class S203 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second‑generation C‑Class estate — from the supercharged M111 and M271 four‑cylinders to the V6 petrol range and the OM611/OM646 diesel family. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the facelift that brought new engines and improved reliability. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes C‑Class S203 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S203 estate specifically — including the pre‑ and post‑facelift engine changes that define this generation's ownership story.

overview.intro: The S203 is the estate body of Mercedes‑Benz's second‑generation C‑Class, arriving in 2001 as the successor to the S202. It introduced the 'four‑eyed' twin‑headlight design language, a longer wheelbase, and a more extensive standard equipment list than its rivals. A significant 2004 facelift brought revised styling, new engine families (M271 petrol, OM646/OM648 diesel), and the arrival of the C 55 AMG V8. The S203 shares its platform and engines with the W203 saloon but offers up to 1,500 litres of load space.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: W203 (Mercedes‑Benz second‑generation C‑Class platform) • Predecessor: S202 (first‑generation C‑Class estate) • Successor: S204 (third‑generation C‑Class estate) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W203 (saloon), CL203 (SportCoupé)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The S203 estate generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM611/OM646 diesel and M271 supercharged petrol variants as these cars enter their third decade of service.

items.quote: “The facelift OM646 improved on the OM611 with better injector design and common‑rail refinement. 150 PS and 340 Nm of torque deliver effortless estate‑car performance with genuine 200,000+ mile potential.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want a proven, efficient diesel estate without the 5‑cylinder’s higher running costs.

“The M112 V6 is the S203's most robust petrol engine — smooth, torquey, and free of the M271's camshaft adjuster issues and the M272's balance shaft concerns. The 218 PS C 320 is the sweet spot.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want V6 refinement without AMG running costs.

“The M272's balance shaft sprocket wear is this generation's defining risk — a design flaw that can lead to catastrophic engine failure. Mercedes acknowledged the issue but didn't extend the warranty as widely as with the M260.” Who it’s for: Buyers who understand the risk and have documented balance shaft history.

“The 367 PS 5.4‑litre V8 is a joy to drive but punishing to repair — valve cover gaskets, timing chain tensioners, and the entire engine bay's heat‑related wear add up fast. A full replacement runs well into five figures.” Who it’s for: AMG enthusiasts with deep pockets and full service history.

“The older OM611 is cheaper to buy and cheaper to replace than the facelift OM646 — and with injector seal maintenance, it's equally durable. 143 PS and 340 Nm are plenty for estate duties.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want diesel economy on a tighter budget.

“The 170 PS C 240 is often dismissed in favour of the C 320, but it shares the same robust M112 V6 architecture with lower running costs — and none of the M272's balance shaft anxiety.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want V6 ownership without the V8 tax.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain tensioner / valve cover gaskets

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — budget for balance shaft work

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve cover gaskets / timing chain wear

economicsRule.title: The S203 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200 OM646 recon against a £5,000 S203 sits at 64% — that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold. Against a tired £3,500 S203, the same recon is over 90% — walk away. A £2,800 M112 V6 recon against a well‑kept £6,000 example is 47% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S203 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM611 (pre‑facelift C 220 CDI) — cheap injector seal maintenance, proven common‑rail diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S203 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: C 55 AMG (M113 V8) — valve cover gaskets, timing chain wear, and V8 complexity add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S203 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M112 V6 (C 240 / C 320) — six‑cylinder refinement without the M272's balance‑shaft anxiety.

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Sprocket Wear (C 230 / C 350)
problems.rootCause: The M272 V6's balance shaft sprocket has a design flaw — the sprocket wears prematurely, causing timing chain slack, camshaft position sensor codes, and eventual engine failure if ignored. Mercedes acknowledged the issue but did not issue a recall.
problems.recommendation: If your S203 C 230 or C 350 develops a check engine light with camshaft position codes, get the balance shaft inspected immediately — a £1,500–£3,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,500–£6,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your S203 C 350 →

problems.title: OM611 / OM646 Injector Seal Failure ('Black Death')
problems.rootCause: The injector seals on OM611 and OM646 engines degrade over time, allowing combustion gases to leak past the seals. This carbonises around the injector, causing the classic 'Black Death' — a tapping sound, loss of power, and eventually injector seizure if ignored.
problems.recommendation: If your S203 C 220 CDI develops a tapping sound or a loss of power, get the injector seals checked immediately — a £400–£1,200 seal replacement is far cheaper than the £1,800–£3,200 replacement that follows a seized injector.

cta.label: Compare OM646 engine prices for your S203 C 220 CDI →

problems.title: M271 Camshaft Adjuster Wear (C 180K / C 200K / C 230K)
problems.affectedModels: S203 C 180 Kompressor, C 200 Kompressor, C 230 Kompressor (2004–2007).
problems.rootCause: The M271's camshaft adjuster mechanism wears prematurely, causing timing chain rattle on start‑up, reduced power, and check engine lights. The issue is well‑documented across the M271 engine family.
problems.recommendation: If your S203 C 180 Kompressor or C 200 Kompressor develops a timing chain rattle on cold start, get the camshaft adjuster inspected immediately — a £600–£1,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,000–£3,800 replacement that follows a jumped timing chain.

cta.label: Compare M271 engine prices for your S203 C 200 Kompressor →

problems.title: M113 Valve Cover Gaskets & Timing Chain Wear (C 55 AMG)
problems.rootCause: The M113 V8 generates significant heat in the engine bay, causing valve cover gaskets to harden and leak oil. Timing chain tensioner wear is also a known issue on higher‑mileage examples.
problems.recommendation: If you own an S203 C 55 AMG, treat valve cover gasket and timing chain tensioner inspections as annual maintenance — £800–£2,000 now is far cheaper than the £4,500–£8,000 replacement that follows a neglected oil leak or timing failure.

cta.label: Compare M113 engine prices for your S203 C 55 AMG →

replacementCosts.h2: S203 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline figures — S203 estate shares identical engine‑bay access with its W203 saloon sibling, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S203 estate only. W203 saloon and CL203 SportCoupé share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.
